title: Cat Vomiting Dark Coffee Ground Substance: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Vomiting dark, coffee-ground material indicates internal bleeding in cats. This is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
What Is It?

When a cat vomits material that resembles dark, grainy coffee grounds, it is a clinical sign of digested blood, known as hematemesis. The granular appearance occurs because the stomach acid has partially digested the blood, causing it to clot and darken. This indicates that bleeding is occurring in the upper gastrointestinal tract, such as the esophagus, stomach, or the upper part of the small intestine.

Is It an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this condition is High. This is considered a medical emergency because internal bleeding can quickly lead to life-threatening complications such as anemia, shock, or organ failure. Common causes include gastric ulcers, ingestion of toxins (like rat poison), foreign body obstructions, or severe inflammatory diseases. You should contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe and fast to do so, take a clear photo of the vomit before cleaning it up. Showing this photo to the veterinary team allows them to visualize the color, consistency, and volume of the blood. This visual evidence provides critical information that helps the staff prioritize your cat for immediate care and helps the doctor narrow down the potential source of the bleeding more quickly.

faqs.question: Could this just be something my cat ate?
faqs.answer: While some dark-colored foods can mimic this appearance, the risk of internal bleeding is too high to ignore. It is vital to treat any coffee-ground-like vomit as a medical emergency until a veterinarian confirms otherwise.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Look for pale gums, extreme lethargy, black tarry stools, or a refusal to eat. However, even if your cat is acting normally, the presence of digested blood requires immediate professional evaluation.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat an antacid at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications or home remedies to a vomiting cat, as many can be toxic or may interfere with the emergency treatments the veterinarian needs to provide.
